role_summary: SNCC voter-registration worker in McComb, Greenwood, and Hattiesburg, Mississippi; arrested with Fannie Lou Hamer at the Winona bus station in June 1963; Tougaloo College student after the Voting Rights Act.
ai_reading: Simpson's testimony sits with a tight cluster of Mississippi voter-registration colleagues in the embedding. Her top six semantic neighbors include Charles McLaurin (Sunflower County project director, entry 17), Frankye Adams-Johnson (entry 43), the joint Ladner sisters interview (entry 75), and Peggy Jean Connor (entry 96), all Mississippi-side SNCC organizers; the embedding therefore confirms her cultural placement inside the Mississippi project network. Her position on the Southern Struggle vs Northern Struggle concept axis is +0.47, far toward the Southern pole, the second-most-Southern reading among the audited interviewees built so far in this catalog and consistent with her geography of work (McComb, Greenwood, Hattiesburg, Jackson). Where the embedding offers something the cultural record alone would not have made obvious is the comparatively secular framing the vector picks up: at +0.40 along Sacred Framing vs Secular Framing, Simpson's testimony reads more toward the secular-political end than the Mississippi voter-registration scene's heavily Black-Baptist context would predict, possibly an artifact of the singing-as-coping memory dominating the religious vocabulary in her transcript.
biographical_paragraph: Simpson was seventeen when she joined SNCC in 1962, the conversation that approved her involvement having taken place between her father and SNCC field secretary Lawrence Guyot [src: 1]. Itta Bena, Mississippi was where she was raised; she was born December 12, 1945 [src: 1]. From the Greenwood Council of Federated Organizations office she did voter-registration work that ran through McComb, Greenwood, and Hattiesburg, and during Freedom Summer in 1964 she worked from the Jackson COFO office and helped coordinate the Freedom Vote campaign [src: 1]. The episode that fixed her in the documentary record came in June 1963, when she, Fannie Lou Hamer, June Johnson, and Annell Ponder were arrested at a bus station in Winona, Mississippi; while incarcerated, Simpson tended to Hamer after the beating Hamer suffered in custody, applying cold towels to Hamer's forehead [src: 1]. She used singing in jail as a way through [src: 1]. After the Voting Rights Act passed she enrolled at Tougaloo College in 1965 with scholarship funding SNCC helped arrange [src: 1]. Her Library of Congress oral history is entry 41 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 2].

sources.title: Euvester Simpson oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)
